**Forensic science**, often confused with **criminalistics**,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) is the application of [science](/wiki/Science "Science") principles and methods to support decision-making related to rules or [law](/wiki/Law "Law"), generally [criminal](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law") and [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)").

During [criminal investigation](/wiki/Criminal_investigation "Criminal investigation") in particular, it is governed by the legal standards of [admissible evidence](/wiki/Admissible_evidence "Admissible evidence") and [criminal procedure](/wiki/Criminal_procedure "Criminal procedure"). It is a broad field utilizing numerous practices such as the analysis of [DNA](/wiki/Genetic_analysis "Genetic analysis"), [fingerprints](/wiki/Fingerprint "Fingerprint"), [bloodstain patterns](/wiki/Bloodstain_pattern_analysis "Bloodstain pattern analysis"), [firearms](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm"), [ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics"), [toxicology](/wiki/Toxicology "Toxicology"), [microscopy](/wiki/Microscopy "Microscopy"), and [fire debris analysis](/wiki/Fire_investigation "Fire investigation"). Modern forensic analysis is also conducted on [cybersecurity](/wiki/Cybersecurity "Cybersecurity") related incidents where major breach has occurred leading to substantial financial loss.

Forensic scientists collect, preserve, and analyze [evidence](/wiki/Evidence "Evidence") during the course of an investigation. While some forensic scientists travel to the scene of the crime to collect the evidence themselves, others occupy a laboratory role, performing analysis on objects brought to them by other individuals.[[3]](#cite_note-job_desc-3) Others are involved in analysis of financial, banking, or other numerical data for use in financial crime investigation, and can be employed as consultants from private firms, academia, or as government employees.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In addition to their laboratory role, forensic scientists testify as [expert witnesses](/wiki/Expert_witness "Expert witness") in both [criminal](/wiki/Criminal_procedure "Criminal procedure") and [civil](/wiki/Lawsuit "Lawsuit") cases and can work for either the [prosecution](/wiki/Prosecutor "Prosecutor") or the defense. While any field could technically be *forensic*, certain sections have developed over time to encompass the majority of forensically related cases.[[5]](#cite_note-aafs_sections-5)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The term *forensic* stems from the Latin word, *forēnsis* (3rd declension, adjective), meaning "of a forum, place of assembly".[[6]](#cite_note-6) The history of the term originates in Roman times, where many judicial processes, such as trials and preliminary hearings, were held in the forum. This origin is the source of the two modern usages of the word *forensic*—as a form of legal evidence; and as a category of public presentation.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In modern use, the term *forensics* is often used in place of "forensic science."

The word "[science](/wiki/Science#Etymology "Science")", is derived from the Latin word for 'knowledge' and is today closely tied to the [scientific method](/wiki/Scientific_method "Scientific method"), a systematic way of acquiring knowledge. Taken together, forensic science means the use of scientific methods and processes for crime solving.

### Origins of forensic science and early methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Origins of forensic science and early methods")]

Main article: [Forensics in antiquity](/wiki/Forensics_in_antiquity "Forensics in antiquity")

The [ancient world](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") lacked standardized forensic practices, which enabled criminals to escape punishment. Criminal investigations and trials relied heavily on forced [confessions](/wiki/Confession_(law) "Confession (law)") and witness [testimony](/wiki/Testimony "Testimony"). However, ancient sources do contain several accounts of techniques that foreshadow concepts in forensic science developed centuries later.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The first written account of using [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") and [entomology](/wiki/Entomology "Entomology") to solve criminal cases is attributed to the book of [*Xi Yuan Lu*](/wiki/Collected_Cases_of_Injustice_Rectified "Collected Cases of Injustice Rectified") (translated as *Washing Away of Wrongs*[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)), written in China in 1248 by [Song Ci](/wiki/Song_Ci "Song Ci") (宋慈, 1186–1249), a director of justice, jail and supervision,[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight3-11) during the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty").

Song Ci introduced regulations concerning autopsy reports to court,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight161-12) how to protect the evidence in the examining process, and explained why forensic workers must demonstrate impartiality to the public.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight76–82-13) He devised methods for making antiseptic and for promoting the reappearance of hidden injuries to dead bodies and bones (using sunlight and vinegar under a red-oil umbrella);[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight95-14) for calculating the time of death (allowing for weather and insect activity);[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight86-15) described how to wash and examine the dead body to ascertain the reason for death.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight87-16) At that time the book had described methods for distinguishing between suicide and faked suicide.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight79–85-17) He wrote the book on forensics stating that all wounds or dead bodies should be examined, not avoided. The book became the first form of literature to help determine the cause of death.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In one of Song Ci's accounts (*Washing Away of Wrongs*), the case of a person murdered with a sickle was solved by an investigator who instructed each suspect to bring his sickle to one location. (He realized it was a sickle by testing various blades on an animal carcass and comparing the wounds.) Flies, attracted by the smell of blood, eventually gathered on a single sickle. In light of this, the owner of that sickle confessed to the murder. The book also described how to distinguish between a [drowning](/wiki/Drowning "Drowning") (water in the [lungs](/wiki/Lungs "Lungs")) and [strangulation](/wiki/Strangulation "Strangulation") (broken neck [cartilage](/wiki/Cartilage "Cartilage")), and described evidence from examining corpses to determine if a death was caused by murder, suicide or accident.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESongMcKnight-19)

Methods from around the world involved saliva and examination of the mouth and tongue to determine innocence or guilt, as a precursor to the [Polygraph test](/wiki/Polygraph_test "Polygraph test"). In ancient India,[[20]](#cite_note-EDodD-20) some suspects were made to fill their mouths with dried rice and spit it back out. Similarly, in ancient [China](/wiki/China "China"), those accused of a crime would have rice powder placed in their mouths.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In ancient [middle-eastern](/wiki/Middle-East "Middle-East") cultures, the accused were made to lick hot metal rods briefly. It is thought that these tests had some validity[[22]](#cite_note-22) since a guilty person would produce less saliva and thus have a drier mouth;[[23]](#cite_note-23) the accused would be considered guilty if rice was sticking to their mouths in abundance or if their tongues were severely burned due to lack of shielding from saliva.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Education and training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Education and training")]

Initial glance, forensic intelligence may appear as a nascent facet of forensic science facilitated by advancements in information technologies such as computers, databases, and data-flow management software. However, a more profound examination reveals that forensic intelligence represents a genuine and emerging inclination among forensic practitioners to actively participate in investigative and policing strategies. In doing so, it elucidates existing practices within scientific literature, advocating for a paradigm shift from the prevailing conception of forensic science as a conglomerate of disciplines merely aiding the criminal justice system. Instead, it urges a perspective that views forensic science as a discipline studying the informative potential of traces—remnants of criminal activity. Embracing this transformative shift poses a significant challenge for education, necessitating a shift in learners' mindset to accept concepts and methodologies in forensic intelligence.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Recent calls advocating for the integration of forensic scientists into the criminal justice system, as well as policing and intelligence missions, underscore the necessity for the establishment of educational and training initiatives in the field of forensic intelligence. This article contends that a discernible gap exists between the perceived and actual comprehension of forensic intelligence among law enforcement and forensic science managers, positing that this asymmetry can be rectified only through educational interventions.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The primary challenge in forensic intelligence education and training is identified as the formulation of programs aimed at heightening awareness, particularly among managers, to mitigate the risk of making suboptimal decisions in information processing. The paper highlights two recent European courses as exemplars of educational endeavors, elucidating lessons learned and proposing future directions.

The overarching conclusion is that the heightened focus on forensic intelligence has the potential to rejuvenate a proactive approach to forensic science, enhance quantifiable efficiency, and foster greater involvement in investigative and managerial decision-making. A novel educational challenge is articulated for forensic science university programs worldwide: a shift in emphasis from a fragmented criminal trace analysis to a more comprehensive security problem-solving approach.

### Development of forensic science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development of forensic science")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Ambroise_Par%C3%A9.jpg/250px-Ambroise_Par%C3%A9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ambroise_Par%C3%A9.jpg)

[Ambroise Paré](/wiki/Ambroise_Par%C3%A9 "Ambroise Paré")'s surgical work laid the groundwork for the development of forensic techniques in the following centuries.

In 16th-century Europe, medical practitioners in army and university settings began to gather information on the cause and [manner of death](/wiki/Manner_of_death "Manner of death"). [Ambroise Paré](/wiki/Ambroise_Par%C3%A9 "Ambroise Paré"), a French army [surgeon](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery"), systematically studied the effects of violent death on internal organs.[[27]](#cite_note-GAB-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Two [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people") surgeons, Fortunato Fidelis and Paolo Zacchia, laid the foundation of modern [pathology](/wiki/Pathology "Pathology") by studying changes that occurred in the structure of the body as the result of disease.[[29]](#cite_note-THSC-29) In the late 18th century, writings on these topics began to appear. These included *A Treatise on Forensic Medicine and Public Health* by the French physician [François-Emmanuel Fodéré](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Emmanuel_Fod%C3%A9r%C3%A9 "François-Emmanuel Fodéré")[[30]](#cite_note-HoFM-30) and *The Complete System of Police Medicine* by the German medical expert [Johann Peter Frank](/wiki/Johann_Peter_Frank "Johann Peter Frank").[[31]](#cite_note-MaSiEM-31)

As the rational values of the [Enlightenment era](/wiki/Enlightenment_era "Enlightenment era") increasingly permeated society in the 18th century, criminal investigation became a more evidence-based, rational procedure − the use of torture to force confessions was curtailed, and belief in witchcraft and other powers of the [occult](/wiki/Occult "Occult") largely ceased to influence the court's decisions. Two examples of English forensic science in individual legal proceedings demonstrate the increasing use of [logic](/wiki/Logic "Logic") and procedure in criminal investigations at the time. In 1784, in [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_Lancashire "Lancaster, Lancashire"), John Toms was tried and convicted for murdering Edward Culshaw with a [pistol](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol"). When the dead body of Culshaw was examined, a pistol wad (crushed paper used to secure powder and balls in the muzzle) found in his head wound matched perfectly with a torn newspaper found in Toms's pocket, leading to the [conviction](/wiki/Conviction "Conviction").[[32]](#cite_note-SW-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Diego_Alva_-_Forensics_Final_Project_%284%29.jpg/500px-Diego_Alva_-_Forensics_Final_Project_%284%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diego_Alva_-_Forensics_Final_Project_(4).jpg)

This is an example and explanation of extractor/ejector marks on casings.

In [Warwick](/wiki/Warwick "Warwick") 1816, a farm laborer was tried and convicted of the murder of a young maidservant. She had been drowned in a shallow pool and bore the marks of violent assault. The police found footprints and an impression from corduroy cloth with a sewn patch in the damp earth near the pool. There were also scattered grains of [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") and chaff. The breeches of a farm labourer who had been threshing wheat nearby were examined and corresponded exactly to the impression in the earth near the pool.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

An article appearing in [Scientific American](/wiki/Scientific_American "Scientific American") in 1885 describes the use of [microscopy](/wiki/Microscopy "Microscopy") to distinguish between the blood of two persons in a criminal case in Chicago.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Chromatography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chromatography")]

[Chromatography](/wiki/Chromatography "Chromatography") is a common technique used in the field of Forensic Science. Chromatography is a method of separating the components of a mixture from a mobile phase.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Chromatography is an essential tool used in forensic science, helping analysts identify and compare trace amounts of samples including ignitable liquids, drugs, and biological samples. Many laboratories utilize [gas chromatography](/wiki/Gas_chromatography "Gas chromatography")/[mass spectrometry](/wiki/Mass_spectrometry "Mass spectrometry") (GC/MS) to examine these kinds of samples; this analysis provides rapid and reliant data to identify samples in question.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Toxicology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Toxicology")]

A method for detecting arsenious oxide, simple [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic"), in corpses was devised in 1773 by the Swedish chemist, [Carl Wilhelm Scheele](/wiki/Carl_Wilhelm_Scheele "Carl Wilhelm Scheele").[[37]](#cite_note-DPaC-37) His work was expanded upon, in 1806, by German chemist Valentin Ross, who learned to detect the poison in the walls of a victim's stomach.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Toxicology, a subfield of forensic chemistry, focuses on detecting and identifying drugs, poisons, and other toxic substances in biological samples. Forensic toxicologists work on cases involving drug overdoses, poisoning, and substance abuse. Their work is critical in determining whether harmful substances play a role in a person's death or impairment. [read more](https://www.dseodisha.com/forensic-science-exploring-key-types/)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Marsh_test_apparatus.jpg/250px-Marsh_test_apparatus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marsh_test_apparatus.jpg)

Apparatus for the arsenic test, devised by [James Marsh](/wiki/James_Marsh_(chemist) "James Marsh (chemist)")

[James Marsh](/wiki/James_Marsh_(chemist) "James Marsh (chemist)") was the first to apply this new science to the art of forensics. He was called by the prosecution in a murder trial to give evidence as a chemist in 1832. The defendant, John Bodle, was accused of poisoning his grandfather with arsenic-laced coffee. Marsh performed the standard test by mixing a suspected sample with [hydrogen sulfide](/wiki/Hydrogen_sulfide "Hydrogen sulfide") and [hydrochloric acid](/wiki/Hydrochloric_acid "Hydrochloric acid"). While he was able to detect arsenic as yellow [arsenic trisulfide](/wiki/Arsenic_trisulfide "Arsenic trisulfide"), when it was shown to the jury it had deteriorated, allowing the suspect to be acquitted due to reasonable doubt.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Annoyed by that, Marsh developed a much better test. He combined a sample containing arsenic with [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") and arsenic-free [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc"), resulting in [arsine](/wiki/Arsine "Arsine") gas. The gas was ignited, and it decomposed to pure metallic arsenic, which, when passed to a cold surface, would appear as a silvery-black deposit.[[40]](#cite_note-40) So sensitive was the test, known formally as the [Marsh test](/wiki/Marsh_test "Marsh test"), that it could detect as little as one-fiftieth of a milligram of arsenic. He first described this test in *The Edinburgh Philosophical Journal* in 1836.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Ballistics and firearms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ballistics and firearms")]

Main article: [Forensic firearm examination](/wiki/Forensic_firearm_examination "Forensic firearm examination")

Ballistics is "the science of the motion of projectiles in flight".[[42]](#cite_note-42) In forensic science, analysts examine the patterns left on bullets and cartridge casings after being ejected from a weapon. When fired, a bullet is left with indentations and markings that are unique to the barrel and firing pin of the firearm that ejected the bullet. This examination can help scientists identify possible makes and models of weapons connected to a crime.

Henry Goddard at [Scotland Yard](/wiki/Scotland_Yard "Scotland Yard") pioneered the use of bullet comparison in 1835. He noticed a flaw in the bullet that killed the victim and was able to trace this back to the mold that was used in the manufacturing process.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Anthropometry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Anthropometry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Bertillon_-_Signalement_Anthropometrique.png/250px-Bertillon_-_Signalement_Anthropometrique.png)](/wiki/File:Bertillon_-_Signalement_Anthropometrique.png)

[Frontispiece](/wiki/Book_frontispiece "Book frontispiece") from [Bertillon's](/wiki/Alphonse_Bertillon "Alphonse Bertillon") *Identification anthropométrique* (1893), demonstrating the measurements needed for his anthropometric identification system

The French police officer [Alphonse Bertillon](/wiki/Alphonse_Bertillon "Alphonse Bertillon") was the first to apply the anthropological technique of [anthropometry](/wiki/Anthropometry "Anthropometry") to law enforcement, thereby creating an identification system based on physical measurements. Before that time, criminals could be identified only by name or photograph.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Thompson_2007-45) Dissatisfied with the *ad hoc* methods used to identify captured criminals in France in the 1870s, he began his work on developing a reliable system of anthropometrics for human classification.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Bertillon created many other [forensics](/wiki/Forensics "Forensics") techniques, including [forensic document examination](/wiki/Forensic_document_examination "Forensic document examination"), the use of [galvanoplastic](/wiki/Galvanoplastic "Galvanoplastic") compounds to preserve [footprints](/wiki/Footprint "Footprint"), [ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics"), and the [dynamometer](/wiki/Dynamometer "Dynamometer"), used to determine the degree of force used in [breaking and entering](/wiki/Burglary "Burglary"). Although his central methods were soon to be supplanted by [fingerprinting](/wiki/Fingerprinting "Fingerprinting"), "his other contributions like the [mug shot](/wiki/Mug_shot "Mug shot") and the systematization of crime-scene photography remain in place to this day."[[45]](#cite_note-Thompson_2007-45)

### Fingerprints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fingerprints")]

Sir [William Herschel](/wiki/Sir_William_Herschel,_2nd_Baronet "Sir William Herschel, 2nd Baronet") was one of the first to advocate the use of fingerprinting in the identification of criminal suspects. While working for the [Indian Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service_(British_India) "Indian Civil Service (British India)"), he began to use thumbprints on documents as a security measure to prevent the then-rampant repudiation of signatures in 1858.[[47]](#cite_note-Herschel1916-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Fingerprints_taken_by_William_James_Herschel_1859-1860.jpg/250px-Fingerprints_taken_by_William_James_Herschel_1859-1860.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fingerprints_taken_by_William_James_Herschel_1859-1860.jpg)

Fingerprints taken by [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") 1859/60

In 1877 at Hooghly (near Kolkata), Herschel instituted the use of fingerprints on contracts and deeds, and he registered government pensioners' fingerprints to prevent the collection of money by relatives after a pensioner's death.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In 1880, [Henry Faulds](/wiki/Henry_Faulds "Henry Faulds"), a Scottish surgeon in a [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") hospital, published his first paper on the subject in the scientific journal *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, discussing the usefulness of fingerprints for identification and proposing a method to record them with printing ink. He established their first classification and was also the first to identify fingerprints left on a vial.[[49]](#cite_note-faulds1-49) Returning to the UK in 1886, he offered the concept to the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") in London, but it was dismissed at that time.[[50]](#cite_note-reid1-50)

Faulds wrote to [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin") with a description of his method, but, too old and ill to work on it, Darwin gave the information to his cousin, [Francis Galton](/wiki/Francis_Galton "Francis Galton"), who was interested in anthropology. Having been thus inspired to study fingerprints for ten years, Galton published a detailed statistical model of fingerprint analysis and identification and encouraged its use in forensic science in his book *Finger Prints*. He had calculated that the chance of a "false positive" (two different individuals having the same fingerprints) was about 1 in 64 billion.[[51]](#cite_note-galtonfps-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Fingerprinting_1928.jpg/250px-Fingerprinting_1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fingerprinting_1928.jpg)

Women clerical employees of the [LA Police Department](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") getting [fingerprinted](/wiki/Fingerprint "Fingerprint") and photographed in 1928

[Juan Vucetich](/wiki/Juan_Vucetich "Juan Vucetich"), an Argentine chief police officer, created the first method of recording the fingerprints of individuals on file. In 1892, after studying Galton's pattern types, Vucetich set up the world's first fingerprint bureau. In that same year, Francisca Rojas of [Necochea](/wiki/Necochea "Necochea") was found in a house with neck injuries whilst her two sons were found dead with their throats cut. Rojas accused a neighbour, but despite brutal interrogation, this neighbour would not confess to the crimes. Inspector Alvarez, a colleague of Vucetich, went to the scene and found a bloody thumb mark on a door. When it was compared with Rojas' prints, it was found to be identical with her right thumb. She then confessed to the murder of her sons.

A Fingerprint Bureau was established in Calcutta ([Kolkata](/wiki/Kolkata "Kolkata")), India, in 1897, after the Council of the Governor General approved a committee report that fingerprints should be used for the classification of criminal records. Working in the Calcutta Anthropometric Bureau, before it became the Fingerprint Bureau, were [Azizul Haque](/wiki/Azizul_Haque_(police_officer) "Azizul Haque (police officer)") and [Hem Chandra Bose](/wiki/Hem_Chandra_Bose "Hem Chandra Bose"). Haque and Bose were Indian fingerprint experts who have been credited with the primary development of a fingerprint classification system eventually named after their supervisor, [Sir Edward Richard Henry](/wiki/Edward_Henry "Edward Henry").[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) The [Henry Classification System](/wiki/Henry_Classification_System "Henry Classification System"), co-devised by Haque and Bose, was accepted in England and Wales when the first United Kingdom Fingerprint Bureau was founded in [Scotland Yard](/wiki/Scotland_Yard "Scotland Yard"), the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") headquarters, London, in 1901. Sir Edward Richard Henry subsequently achieved improvements in dactyloscopy.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In the United States, Henry P. DeForrest used fingerprinting in the [New York Civil Service](/wiki/New_York_Police_Department "New York Police Department") in 1902, and by December 1905, [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") Deputy Commissioner Joseph A. Faurot, an expert in the [Bertillon](/wiki/Alphonse_Bertillon "Alphonse Bertillon") system and a fingerprint advocate at Police Headquarters, introduced the fingerprinting of criminals to the United States.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Uhlenhuth test

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Uhlenhuth test")]

The [Uhlenhuth test](/wiki/Uhlenhuth_test "Uhlenhuth test"), or the antigen–antibody [precipitin](/wiki/Precipitin "Precipitin") test for species, was invented by [Paul Uhlenhuth](/wiki/Paul_Uhlenhuth "Paul Uhlenhuth") in 1901 and could distinguish human [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") from animal blood, based on the discovery that the blood of different species had one or more characteristic proteins. The test represented a major breakthrough and came to have tremendous importance in forensic science.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The test was further refined for forensic use by the Swiss chemist Maurice Müller in the year 1960s.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### DNA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: DNA")]

Forensic [DNA analysis](/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling") was first used in 1984. It was developed by Sir [Alec Jeffreys](/wiki/Alec_Jeffreys "Alec Jeffreys"), who realized that variation in the genetic sequence could be used to identify individuals and to tell individuals apart from one another. The first application of DNA profiles was used by Jeffreys in a double murder mystery in the small English town of [Narborough, Leicestershire](/wiki/Narborough,_Leicestershire "Narborough, Leicestershire"), in 1985. A 15-year-old school girl by the name of Lynda Mann was raped and murdered in Carlton Hayes psychiatric hospital. The police did not find a suspect but were able to obtain a semen sample.

In 1986, Dawn Ashworth, 15 years old, was also raped and strangled in the nearby village of [Enderby](/wiki/Enderby,_Leicestershire "Enderby, Leicestershire"). Forensic evidence showed that both killers had the same blood type. Richard Buckland became the suspect because he worked at Carlton Hayes psychiatric hospital, had been spotted near Dawn Ashworth's murder scene and knew unreleased details about the body. He later confessed to Dawn's murder but not Lynda's. Jefferys was brought into the case to analyze the semen samples. He concluded that there was no match between the samples and Buckland, who became the first person to be exonerated using DNA. Jefferys confirmed that the DNA profiles were identical for the two murder semen samples. To find the perpetrator, DNA samples from the entire male population, more than 4,000 aged from 17 to 34, of the town were collected. They all were compared to semen samples from the crime. A friend of [Colin Pitchfork](/wiki/Colin_Pitchfork "Colin Pitchfork") was heard saying that he had given his sample to the police claiming to be Colin. Colin Pitchfork was arrested in 1987 and it was found that his DNA profile matched the semen samples from the murder.

Because of this case, DNA databases were developed. There is the national (FBI) and international databases as well as the European countries (ENFSI: European Network of Forensic Science Institutes). These searchable databases are used to match crime scene DNA profiles to those already in a database.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Maturation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Maturation")]

[![Cartoon of a man holding a bloody knife looking contemptuously at a display of half-a-dozen supposed and dissimilar likenesses](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/JacktheRipperPuck.jpg/250px-JacktheRipperPuck.jpg)](/wiki/File:JacktheRipperPuck.jpg)

Police brought to bear the latest techniques of forensic science in their attempts to identify and capture the serial killer [Jack the Ripper](/wiki/Jack_the_Ripper "Jack the Ripper").

By the turn of the 20th century, the science of forensics had become largely established in the sphere of criminal investigation. Scientific and surgical investigation was widely employed by the [Metropolitan Police](/wiki/Metropolitan_Police "Metropolitan Police") during their pursuit of the mysterious [Jack the Ripper](/wiki/Jack_the_Ripper "Jack the Ripper"), who had killed a number of women in the 1880s. This case is a watershed in the application of forensic science. Large teams of policemen conducted house-to-house inquiries throughout Whitechapel. Forensic material was collected and examined. Suspects were identified, traced and either examined more closely or eliminated from the inquiry. Police work follows the same pattern today.[[59]](#cite_note-canter12-59) Over 2000 people were interviewed, "upwards of 300" people were investigated, and 80 people were detained.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

The investigation was initially conducted by the [Criminal Investigation Department](/wiki/Criminal_Investigation_Department "Criminal Investigation Department") (CID), headed by Detective Inspector [Edmund Reid](/wiki/Edmund_Reid "Edmund Reid"). Later, Detective Inspectors [Frederick Abberline](/wiki/Frederick_Abberline "Frederick Abberline"), [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore_(police_officer) "Henry Moore (police officer)"), and [Walter Andrews](/wiki/Walter_Simon_Andrews "Walter Simon Andrews") were sent from Central Office at [Scotland Yard](/wiki/Scotland_Yard "Scotland Yard") to assist. Initially, butchers, surgeons and physicians were suspected because of the manner of the mutilations. The alibis of local butchers and slaughterers were investigated, with the result that they were eliminated from the inquiry.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Some contemporary figures thought the pattern of the murders indicated that the culprit was a butcher or cattle drover on one of the cattle boats that plied between London and mainland Europe. Whitechapel was close to the [London Docks](/wiki/London_Docks "London Docks"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) and usually such boats docked on Thursday or Friday and departed on Saturday or Sunday.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The cattle boats were examined, but the dates of the murders did not coincide with a single boat's movements, and the transfer of a crewman between boats was also ruled out.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

At the end of October, Robert Anderson asked police surgeon [Thomas Bond](/wiki/Thomas_Bond_(British_physician) "Thomas Bond (British physician)") to give his opinion on the extent of the murderer's surgical skill and knowledge.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The opinion offered by Bond on the character of the "Whitechapel murderer" is the earliest surviving [offender profile](/wiki/Offender_profiling "Offender profiling").[[66]](#cite_note-canter5-66) Bond's assessment was based on his own examination of the most extensively mutilated victim and the [post mortem](/wiki/Autopsy "Autopsy") notes from the four previous canonical murders.[[67]](#cite_note-bond-67) In his opinion the killer must have been a man of solitary habits, subject to "periodical attacks of homicidal and erotic [mania](/wiki/Mania "Mania")", with the character of the mutilations possibly indicating "[satyriasis](/wiki/Satyriasis "Satyriasis")".[[67]](#cite_note-bond-67) Bond also stated that "the homicidal impulse may have developed from a revengeful or brooding condition of the mind, or that religious mania may have been the original disease but I do not think either hypothesis is likely".[[67]](#cite_note-bond-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/The_Adventure_of_the_Abbey_Grange_03.jpg/250px-The_Adventure_of_the_Abbey_Grange_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Adventure_of_the_Abbey_Grange_03.jpg)

The popular fictional character [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") was in many ways ahead of his time in his use of forensic analysis.

*Handbook for Coroners, police officials, military policemen* was written by the [Austrian](/wiki/Austrians "Austrians") criminal jurist [Hans Gross](/wiki/Hans_Gross "Hans Gross") in 1893, and is generally acknowledged as the birth of the field of criminalistics. The work combined in one system fields of knowledge that had not been previously integrated, such as psychology and physical science, and which could be successfully used against crime. Gross adapted some fields to the needs of criminal investigation, such as [crime scene photography](/wiki/Crime_scene_photography "Crime scene photography"). He went on to found the Institute of Criminalistics in 1912, as part of the University of Graz' Law School. This Institute was followed by many similar institutes all over the world.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 1909, [Archibald Reiss](/wiki/Archibald_Reiss "Archibald Reiss") founded the *Institut de police scientifique* of the [University of Lausanne (UNIL)](/wiki/University_of_Lausanne "University of Lausanne"), the first school of forensic science in the world. Dr. [Edmond Locard](/wiki/Edmond_Locard "Edmond Locard"), became known as the "[Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") of [France](/wiki/France "France")". He formulated the basic principle of forensic science: "Every contact leaves a trace", which became known as [Locard's exchange principle](/wiki/Locard%27s_exchange_principle "Locard's exchange principle"). In 1910, he founded what may have been the first criminal laboratory in the world, after persuading the Police Department of [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") (France) to give him two attic rooms and two assistants.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Symbolic of the newfound prestige of forensics and the use of reasoning in detective work was the popularity of the fictional character [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes"), written by [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle") in the late 19th century. He remains a great inspiration for forensic science, especially for the way his acute study of a crime scene yielded small [clues](/wiki/Clue_(information) "Clue (information)") as to the precise sequence of events. He made great use of [trace evidence](/wiki/Trace_evidence "Trace evidence") such as shoe and tire impressions, as well as fingerprints, [ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics") and [handwriting analysis](/wiki/Graphology "Graphology"), now known as [questioned document examination](/wiki/Questioned_document_examination "Questioned document examination").[[70]](#cite_note-70) Such evidence is used to test theories conceived by the police, for example, or by the investigator himself.[[71]](#cite_note-71) All of the techniques advocated by Holmes later became reality, but were generally in their infancy at the time Conan Doyle was writing. In many of his reported cases, Holmes frequently complains of the way the crime scene has been contaminated by others, especially by the police, emphasising the critical importance of maintaining its integrity, a now well-known feature of crime scene examination. He used [analytical chemistry](/wiki/Analytical_chemistry "Analytical chemistry") for [blood residue](/wiki/Blood_residue "Blood residue") analysis as well as [toxicology](/wiki/Toxicology "Toxicology") examination and determination for poisons. He used [ballistics](/wiki/Ballistics "Ballistics") by measuring bullet [calibres](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber") and matching them with a suspected murder weapon.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Late 19th – early 20th century figures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Late 19th – early 20th century figures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Shoeprint%28forensic%29.jpg/250px-Shoeprint%28forensic%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shoeprint(forensic).jpg)

Shoeprints have long been used to match a pair of shoes to a crime scene.

[Hans Gross](/wiki/Hans_Gross "Hans Gross") applied scientific methods to crime scenes and was responsible for the birth of criminalistics.

[Edmond Locard](/wiki/Edmond_Locard "Edmond Locard") expanded on Gross' work with [Locard's exchange principle](/wiki/Locard%27s_exchange_principle "Locard's exchange principle") which stated "whenever two objects come into contact with one another, materials are exchanged between them". This means that every contact by a criminal leaves a trace.

[Alexandre Lacassagne](/wiki/Alexandre_Lacassagne "Alexandre Lacassagne"), who taught Locard, produced autopsy standards on actual forensic cases.

[Alphonse Bertillon](/wiki/Alphonse_Bertillon "Alphonse Bertillon") was a French criminologist and founder of [Anthropometry](/wiki/Anthropometry "Anthropometry") (scientific study of measurements and proportions of the human body). He used anthropometry for identification, stating that, since each individual is unique, by measuring aspects of physical difference there could be a personal identification system. He created the Bertillon System around 1879, a way of identifying criminals and citizens by measuring 20 parts of the body. In 1884, over 240 repeat offenders were caught using the Bertillon system, but the system was largely superseded by fingerprinting.

[Joseph Thomas Walker](/wiki/Joseph_Thomas_Walker "Joseph Thomas Walker"), known for his work at [Massachusetts State Police](/wiki/Massachusetts_State_Police "Massachusetts State Police") Chemical Laboratory, for developing many modern forensic techniques which he frequently published in academic journals, and for teaching at the Department of Legal Medicine, [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").

[Frances Glessner Lee](/wiki/Frances_Glessner_Lee "Frances Glessner Lee"), known as "the mother of forensic science",[[73]](#cite_note-73) was instrumental in the development of forensic science in the US. She lobbied to have coroners replaced by medical professionals, endowed the Harvard Associates in Police Science, and conducted many seminars to educate homicide investigators. She also created the [Nutshell Studies of Unexplained Death](/wiki/Nutshell_Studies_of_Unexplained_Death "Nutshell Studies of Unexplained Death"), intricate crime scene dioramas used to train investigators, which are still in use today.

[Mary Louisa Willard](/wiki/Mary_Louisa_Willard "Mary Louisa Willard"), a chemistry professor at [Pennsylvania State University](/wiki/Pennsylvania_State_University "Pennsylvania State University") (Penn State) from the 1920s through early 1960s, used her expertise in [microscopy](/wiki/Microscopy "Microscopy") and chemical forensics to assist local and international criminal investigations.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c5/Alec_Jeffreys_-2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alec_Jeffreys_-2008.jpg)

[Alec Jeffreys](/wiki/Alec_Jeffreys "Alec Jeffreys") invented the [DNA profiling](/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling") technique in 1984.

Later in the 20th century several British pathologists, [Mikey Rochman](/w/index.php?title=Mikey_Rochman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mikey Rochman (page does not exist)"), [Francis Camps](/wiki/Francis_Camps "Francis Camps"), [Sydney Smith](/wiki/Sydney_Smith_(forensic_expert) "Sydney Smith (forensic expert)") and [Keith Simpson](/wiki/Keith_Simpson_(professor) "Keith Simpson (professor)") pioneered new forensic science methods. [Alec Jeffreys](/wiki/Alec_Jeffreys "Alec Jeffreys") pioneered the use of [DNA profiling](/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling") in forensic science in 1984. He realized the scope of DNA fingerprinting, which uses variations in the [genetic code](/wiki/Genetic_code "Genetic code") to identify individuals. The method has since become important in forensic science to assist police detective work, and it has also proved useful in resolving paternity and immigration disputes.[[74]](#cite_note-welcome_2004-74) DNA fingerprinting was first used as a police forensic test to identify the rapist and killer of two teenagers, Lynda Mann and Dawn Ashworth, who were both murdered in [Narborough, Leicestershire](/wiki/Narborough,_Leicestershire "Narborough, Leicestershire"), in 1983 and 1986 respectively. [Colin Pitchfork](/wiki/Colin_Pitchfork "Colin Pitchfork") was identified and convicted of murder after samples taken from him matched [semen](/wiki/Semen "Semen") samples taken from the two dead girls.

Forensic science has been fostered by a number of national and international forensic science learned bodies including the [American Academy of Forensic Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Forensic_Sciences "American Academy of Forensic Sciences") (founded 1948), publishers of the *[Journal of Forensic Sciences](/wiki/Journal_of_Forensic_Sciences "Journal of Forensic Sciences")*;[[75]](#cite_note-75) the [Canadian Society of Forensic Science](/wiki/Canadian_Society_of_Forensic_Science "Canadian Society of Forensic Science") (founded 1953), publishers of the *[Journal of the Canadian Society of Forensic Science](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Canadian_Society_of_Forensic_Science "Journal of the Canadian Society of Forensic Science")*; [the Chartered Society of Forensic Sciences](/wiki/The_Chartered_Society_of_Forensic_Sciences "The Chartered Society of Forensic Sciences"),[[76]](#cite_note-76) (founded 1959), then known as the Forensic Science Society, publisher of *[Science & Justice](/wiki/Science_%26_Justice "Science & Justice")*;[[77]](#cite_note-77) the British Academy of Forensic Sciences[[78]](#cite_note-78) (founded 1960), publishers of *[Medicine, Science and the Law](/wiki/Medicine,_Science_and_the_Law "Medicine, Science and the Law")*;[[79]](#cite_note-79) the [Australian Academy of Forensic Sciences](/wiki/Australian_Academy_of_Forensic_Sciences "Australian Academy of Forensic Sciences") (founded 1967), publishers of the *Australian Journal of Forensic Sciences*; and the [European Network of Forensic Science Institutes](/wiki/European_Network_of_Forensic_Science_Institutes "European Network of Forensic Science Institutes") (founded 1995).

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In the past decade, documenting forensics scenes has become more efficient. Forensic scientists have started using laser scanners, drones and photogrammetry to obtain 3D point clouds of accidents or crime scenes. Reconstruction of an accident scene on a highway using drones involves data acquisition time of only 10–20 minutes and can be performed without shutting down traffic. The results are not just accurate, in centimeters, for measurement to be presented in court but also easy to digitally preserve in the long term.[[80]](#cite_note-80)
Now, in the 21st century, much of forensic science's future is up for discussion. The [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology") (NIST) has several forensic science-related programs: [CSAFE](/wiki/CSAFE "CSAFE"), a NIST Center of Excellence in Forensic Science, the [National Commission on Forensic Science](/w/index.php?title=National_Commission_on_Forensic_Science&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Commission on Forensic Science (page does not exist)") (now concluded), and administration of the Organization of Scientific Area Committees for Forensic Science (OSAC).[[81]](#cite_note-81) One of the more recent additions by NIST is a document called NISTIR-7941, titled "Forensic Science Laboratories: Handbook for Facility Planning, Design, Construction, and Relocation". The handbook provides a clear blueprint for approaching forensic science. The details even include what type of staff should be hired for certain positions.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/US_Army_CID_agents_at_crime_scene.jpg/250px-US_Army_CID_agents_at_crime_scene.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Army_CID_agents_at_crime_scene.jpg)

Agents of the [United States Army Criminal Investigation Division](/wiki/United_States_Army_Criminal_Investigation_Division "United States Army Criminal Investigation Division") investigate a crime scene.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/England-forensic2.jpg/250px-England-forensic2.jpg)](/wiki/File:England-forensic2.jpg)

Police forensic investigation in [Ashton-under-Lyne](/wiki/Ashton-under-Lyne "Ashton-under-Lyne"), England, using a tent to protect the crime scene

* [Art forensics](/wiki/Art_forensics "Art forensics") concerns the art authentication cases to help research the work's authenticity. Art authentication methods are used to detect and identify forgery, faking and copying of art works, e.g. paintings.
* [Bloodstain pattern analysis](/wiki/Bloodstain_pattern_analysis "Bloodstain pattern analysis") is the scientific examination of blood spatter patterns found at a crime scene to reconstruct the events of the crime.
* Comparative forensics is the application of visual comparison techniques to verify similarity of physical evidence. This includes fingerprint analysis, toolmark analysis, and ballistic analysis.
* [Computational forensics](/wiki/Computational_forensics "Computational forensics") concerns the development of algorithms and software to assist forensic examination.
* [Criminalistics](/wiki/Crime_lab "Crime lab") is the application of various sciences to answer questions relating to examination and comparison of [biological evidence](/wiki/Forensic_genetics "Forensic genetics"), [trace evidence](/wiki/Trace_evidence "Trace evidence"), impression evidence (such as [fingerprints](/wiki/Fingerprint "Fingerprint"), [footwear impressions](/wiki/Forensic_footwear_evidence "Forensic footwear evidence"), and [tire tracks](/wiki/Forensic_tire_tread_evidence "Forensic tire tread evidence")), [controlled substances](/wiki/Controlled_substance "Controlled substance"), ballistics, firearm and toolmark examination, and other evidence in criminal investigations. In typical circumstances, evidence is processed in a crime lab.
* [Digital forensics](/wiki/Digital_forensics "Digital forensics") is the application of proven scientific methods and techniques in order to recover data from electronic / digital media. Digital Forensic specialists work in the field as well as in the lab.
* [Ear print analysis](/wiki/Ear_print_analysis "Ear print analysis") is used as a means of forensic identification intended as an identification tool similar to fingerprinting. An earprint is a two-dimensional reproduction of the parts of the outer ear that have touched a specific surface (most commonly the helix, antihelix, tragus and antitragus).
* [Election forensics](/wiki/Election_forensics "Election forensics") is the use of statistics to determine if election results are normal or abnormal. It is also used to look into and detect the cases concerning gerrymandering.
* [Forensic accounting](/wiki/Forensic_accounting "Forensic accounting") is the study and interpretation of accounting evidence, financial statement namely: Balance sheet, Income statement, Cash flow statement.
* Forensic aerial photography is the study and interpretation of aerial photographic evidence.
* [Forensic anthropology](/wiki/Forensic_anthropology "Forensic anthropology") is the application of [physical anthropology](/wiki/Physical_anthropology "Physical anthropology") in a legal setting, usually for the [recovery and identification](/wiki/Forensic_facial_reconstruction "Forensic facial reconstruction") of [skeletonized](/wiki/Skeletonization_(forensics) "Skeletonization (forensics)") human remains.
* [Forensic archaeology](/wiki/Forensic_archaeology "Forensic archaeology") is the application of a combination of [archaeological](/wiki/Archaeological "Archaeological") techniques and forensic science, typically in law enforcement.
* [Forensic astronomy](/wiki/Forensic_astronomy "Forensic astronomy") uses methods from [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") to determine past celestial constellations for forensic purposes.
* [Forensic botany](/wiki/Forensic_botany "Forensic botany") is the study of plant life in order to gain information regarding possible crimes.
* [Forensic chemistry](/wiki/Forensic_chemistry "Forensic chemistry") is the study of detection and identification of [illicit drugs](/wiki/Illicit_drugs "Illicit drugs"), accelerants used in [arson](/wiki/Arson "Arson") cases, explosive and [gunshot residue](/wiki/Gunshot_residue "Gunshot residue").
* [Forensic dactyloscopy](/wiki/Fingerprint "Fingerprint") is the study of [fingerprints](/wiki/Fingerprint "Fingerprint").
* Forensic document examination or [questioned document examination](/wiki/Questioned_document_examination "Questioned document examination") answers questions about a disputed document using a variety of scientific processes and methods. Many examinations involve a comparison of the questioned document, or components of the document, with a set of known standards. The most common type of examination involves handwriting, whereby the examiner tries to address concerns about potential authorship.
* [Forensic DNA analysis](/wiki/DNA_profiling "DNA profiling") takes advantage of the uniqueness of an individual's DNA to answer forensic questions such as [paternity/maternity testing](/wiki/DNA_paternity_testing "DNA paternity testing") and placing a suspect at a crime scene, e.g. in a [rape investigation](/wiki/Rape_investigation "Rape investigation").
* [Forensic engineering](/wiki/Forensic_engineering "Forensic engineering") is the scientific examination and analysis of structures and products relating to their failure or cause of damage.
* [Forensic entomology](/wiki/Forensic_entomology "Forensic entomology") deals with the examination of [insects](/wiki/Insects "Insects") in, on and around human remains to assist in determination of time or location of death. It is also possible to determine if the body was moved after death using entomology.
* [Forensic geology](/wiki/Forensic_geology "Forensic geology") deals with [trace evidence](/wiki/Trace_evidence "Trace evidence") in the form of soils, minerals and petroleum.
* Forensic geomorphology is the study of the ground surface to look for potential location(s) of buried object(s).[[83]](#cite_note-83)
* [Forensic geophysics](/wiki/Forensic_geophysics "Forensic geophysics") is the application of geophysical techniques such as radar for detecting objects hidden underground[[84]](#cite_note-84) or underwater.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* Forensic intelligence process starts with the collection of data and ends with the integration of results within into the analysis of crimes under investigation.[[86]](#cite_note-86)
* Forensic interviews are conducted using the science of professionally using expertise to conduct a variety of investigative interviews with victims, witnesses, suspects or other sources to determine the facts regarding suspicions, allegations or specific incidents in either public or private sector settings.
* Forensic histopathology is the application of histological techniques and examination to forensic pathology practice.
* [Forensic limnology](/wiki/Forensic_limnology "Forensic limnology") is the analysis of evidence collected from crime scenes in or around fresh-water sources. Examination of biological organisms, in particular [diatoms](/wiki/Diatom "Diatom"), can be useful in connecting suspects with victims.
* [Forensic linguistics](/wiki/Forensic_linguistics "Forensic linguistics") deals with issues in the legal system that requires linguistic expertise.
* [Forensic meteorology](/wiki/Forensic_meteorology "Forensic meteorology") is a site-specific analysis of past weather conditions for a point of loss.
* [Forensic metrology](/wiki/Forensic_metrology "Forensic metrology")[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) is the application of metrology to assess the reliability of scientific evidence obtained through measurements
* Forensic microbiology is the study of the [necrobiome](/wiki/Necrobiome "Necrobiome").
* [Forensic nursing](/wiki/Forensic_nursing "Forensic nursing") is the application of [Nursing](/wiki/Nursing "Nursing") sciences to abusive crimes, like child abuse, or sexual abuse. Categorization of wounds and traumas, collection of bodily fluids and emotional support are some of the duties of forensic nurses.
* [Forensic odontology](/wiki/Forensic_odontology "Forensic odontology") is the study of the uniqueness of dentition, better known as the study of teeth.
* Forensic optometry is the study of glasses and other eyewear relating to crime scenes and criminal investigations.
* [Forensic pathology](/wiki/Forensic_pathology "Forensic pathology") is a field in which the principles of [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") and [pathology](/wiki/Pathology "Pathology") are applied to determine a cause of death or injury in the context of a legal inquiry.
* [Forensic podiatry](/wiki/Forensic_podiatry "Forensic podiatry") is an application of the study of feet [footprint](/wiki/Footprint "Footprint") or footwear and their traces to analyze scene of crime and to establish personal identity in forensic examinations.
* [Forensic psychiatry](/wiki/Forensic_psychiatry "Forensic psychiatry") is a specialized branch of [psychiatry](/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") as applied to and based on scientific [criminology](/wiki/Criminology "Criminology").
* [Forensic psychology](/wiki/Forensic_psychology "Forensic psychology") is the study of the mind of an individual, using forensic methods. Usually it determines the circumstances behind a criminal's behavior.
* [Forensic seismology](/wiki/Forensic_seismology "Forensic seismology") is the study of techniques to distinguish the seismic signals generated by underground nuclear explosions from those generated by earthquakes.
* [Forensic serology](/wiki/Forensic_serology "Forensic serology") is the study of the body fluids.[[89]](#cite_note-89)
* [Forensic social work](/wiki/Forensic_social_work "Forensic social work") is the specialist study of [social work](/wiki/Social_work "Social work") theories and their applications to a clinical, criminal justice or [psychiatric](/wiki/Psychiatric "Psychiatric") setting. Practitioners of forensic social work connected with the [criminal justice system](/wiki/Criminal_justice_system "Criminal justice system") are often termed Social Supervisors, whilst the remaining use the interchangeable titles [forensic social worker](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_social_worker&action=edit&redlink=1 "Forensic social worker (page does not exist)"), [approved mental health professional](/wiki/Approved_mental_health_professional "Approved mental health professional") or forensic practitioner and they conduct specialist assessments of risk, care planning and act as an officer of the court.
* [Forensic toxicology](/wiki/Forensic_toxicology "Forensic toxicology") is the study of the effect of [drugs](/wiki/Hard_and_soft_drugs "Hard and soft drugs") and [poisons](/wiki/Poison "Poison") on/in the human body.
* [Forensic video analysis](/wiki/Forensic_video_analysis "Forensic video analysis") is the scientific examination, comparison and evaluation of video in legal matters.
* [Mobile device forensics](/wiki/Mobile_device_forensics "Mobile device forensics") is the scientific examination and evaluation of evidence found in mobile phones, e.g. Call History and Deleted SMS, and includes SIM Card Forensics.
* [Trace evidence](/wiki/Trace_evidence "Trace evidence") analysis is the analysis and comparison of trace evidence including glass, paint, fibres and hair (e.g., using [micro-spectrophotometry](/wiki/Micro-spectrophotometry "Micro-spectrophotometry")).
* [Wildlife forensic science](/wiki/Wildlife_forensic_science "Wildlife forensic science") applies a range of scientific disciplines to legal cases involving non-human biological evidence, to solve crimes such as poaching, [animal abuse](/wiki/Animal_abuse "Animal abuse"), and trade in endangered species.

## Questionable techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Questionable techniques")]

Some forensic techniques, believed to be scientifically sound at the time they were used, have turned out later to have much less scientific merit or none.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Some such techniques include:

* [Comparative bullet-lead analysis](/wiki/Comparative_bullet-lead_analysis "Comparative bullet-lead analysis") was used by the FBI for over four decades, starting with the [John F. Kennedy assassination](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_assassination "John F. Kennedy assassination") in 1963. The theory was that each batch of [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") possessed a chemical makeup so distinct that a bullet could be traced back to a particular batch or even a specific box. Internal studies and an outside study by the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/United_States_National_Academy_of_Sciences "United States National Academy of Sciences") found that the technique was unreliable due to improper interpretation, and the FBI abandoned the test in 2005.[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* [Forensic dentistry](/wiki/Forensic_dentistry "Forensic dentistry") has come under fire: in at least three cases bite-mark evidence has been used to convict people of murder who were later freed by DNA evidence.[[92]](#cite_note-92) A 1999 study by a member of the American Board of Forensic Odontology found a 63 percent rate of false identifications and is commonly referenced within online news stories and conspiracy websites.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) The study was based on an informal workshop during an ABFO meeting, which many members did not consider a valid scientific setting.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The theory is that each person has a unique and distinctive set of teeth, which leave a pattern after biting someone. They analyze the dental characteristics such as size, shape, and arch form.[[96]](#cite_note-96)
* Police Access to Genetic Genealogy Databases: There are privacy concerns with the police being able to access personal genetic data that is on genealogy services.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) Individuals can become criminal informants to their own families or to themselves simply by participating in genetic genealogy databases. The Combined DNA Index System (CODIS) is a database that the FBI uses to hold genetic profiles of all known felons, misdemeanants, and arrestees.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) Some people argue that individuals who are using genealogy databases should have an expectation of privacy in their data that is or may be violated by genetic searches by law enforcement.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) These different services have warning signs about potential third parties using their information, but most individuals do not read the agreement thoroughly. According to a study by Christi Guerrini, Jill Robinson, Devan Petersen, and Amy McGuire, they found that the majority of the people who took the survey support police searches of genetic websites that identify genetic relatives.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) People who responded to the survey are more supportive of police activities using genetic genealogy when it is for the purpose of identifying offenders of violent crimes, suspects of crimes against children or missing people. The data from the surveys that were given show that individuals are not concerned about police searches using personal genetic data if it is justified. It was found in this study that offenders are disproportionally low-income and black and the average person of [genetic testing](/wiki/Genetic_testing "Genetic testing") is wealthy and white. The results from the study had different results.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) In 2016, there was a survey called the National Crime Victimization Survey (NCVS) that was provided by the US Bureau of Justice Statistics. In that survey, it was found that 1.3% of people aged 12 or older were victims of violent crimes, and 8.85 of households were victims of property crimes.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) There were some issues with this survey though. The NCVS produces only the annual estimates of victimization. The survey that Christi Guerrini, Jill Robinson, Devan Petersen, and Amy McGuire produced asked the participants about the incidents of victimization over one's lifetime.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) Their survey also did not restrict other family members to one household.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) Around 25% of people who responded to the survey said that they have had family members that have been employed by law enforcement which includes security guards and bailiffs.[[97]](#cite_note-:0-97) Throughout these surveys, it has been found that there is public support for law enforcement to access genetic genealogy databases.

## Litigation science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Litigation science")]

"Litigation science" describes analysis or data developed or produced *expressly* for use in a trial versus those produced in the course of independent research. This distinction was made by the [U.S. 9th Circuit Court of Appeals](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Ninth_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit") when evaluating the admissibility of experts.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

This uses [demonstrative evidence](/wiki/Demonstrative_evidence "Demonstrative evidence"), which is evidence created in preparation of trial by [attorneys](/wiki/Lawyer "Lawyer") or [paralegals](/wiki/Paralegal "Paralegal").

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 2025, there are currently an estimated 18,500 forensic science technicians in the United States.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Media impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Media impact")]

Real-life crime scene investigators and forensic scientists warn that popular television shows do not give a realistic picture of the work, often wildly distorting its nature, and exaggerating the ease, speed, effectiveness, drama, glamour, influence and comfort level of their jobs—which they describe as far more mundane, tedious and boring.[[100]](#cite_note-how_accurate-100)[[101]](#cite_note-accurate_psu-101)

Some claim these modern TV shows have changed individuals' expectations of forensic science, sometimes unrealistically—an influence termed the "[CSI effect](/wiki/CSI_effect "CSI effect")".[[102]](#cite_note-102)[[103]](#cite_note-alldredge-103)

Further, research has suggested that public misperceptions about criminal forensics can create, in the mind of a [juror](/wiki/Juror "Juror"), unrealistic expectations of forensic evidence—which they expect to see before convicting—implicitly biasing the juror towards the defendant. Citing the "CSI effect," at least one researcher has suggested screening jurors for their level of influence from such TV programs.[[103]](#cite_note-alldredge-103)

Further, [research](https://corescholar.libraries.wright.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3788&context=etd_all) has shown that [newspaper](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") media has been found to shape readers general knowledge and perceptions of science and technology in a rather positive way. It could lead to support of it due to the interest readers may obtain and seek further knowledge on the topic.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Controversies")]

Questions about certain areas of forensic science, such as fingerprint evidence and the assumptions behind these disciplines have been brought to light in some publications[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) including the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")*.[[106]](#cite_note-ForensicChallenge-106) The article stated that "No one has proved even the basic assumption: That everyone's fingerprint is unique."[[106]](#cite_note-ForensicChallenge-106) The article also stated that "Now such assumptions are being questioned—and with it may come a radical change in how forensic science is used by police departments and prosecutors."[[106]](#cite_note-ForensicChallenge-106) Law professor Jessica Gabel said on NOVA that forensic science "lacks the rigors, the standards, the quality controls and procedures that we find, usually, in science".[[107]](#cite_note-107)

The National Institute of Standards and Technology has reviewed the scientific foundations of bite-mark analysis used in forensic science. Bite mark analysis is a forensic science technique that analyzes the marks on the victim's skin compared to the suspects teeth.[[108]](#cite_note-108) NIST reviewed the findings of the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2009 study. The National Academics of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine conducted research to address the issues of reliability, accuracy, and reliability of bitemark analysis, where they concluded that there is a lack of sufficient scientific foundation to support the data.[[109]](#cite_note-nist.gov-109) Yet the technique is still legal to use in court as evidence. NIST funded a 2019 meeting that consisted of dentists, lawyers, researchers and others to address the gaps in this field.[[109]](#cite_note-nist.gov-109)

In the US, on 25 June 2009, the Supreme Court issued a 5-to-4 decision in *[Melendez-Diaz v. Massachusetts](/wiki/Melendez-Diaz_v._Massachusetts "Melendez-Diaz v. Massachusetts")* stating that crime laboratory reports may not be used against criminal defendants at trial unless the analysts responsible for creating them give testimony and subject themselves to cross-examination.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The Supreme Court cited the National Academies of Sciences report *Strengthening Forensic Science in the United States*[[111]](#cite_note-Nap.edu-2009-111) in their decision. Writing for the majority, Justice [Antonin Scalia](/wiki/Antonin_Scalia "Antonin Scalia") referred to the [National Research Council](/wiki/United_States_National_Research_Council "United States National Research Council") report in his assertion that "Forensic evidence is not uniquely immune from the risk of manipulation."

In the US, another area of forensic science that has come under question in recent years is the lack of laws requiring the accreditation of forensic labs. Some states require accreditation, but some states do not. Because of this,[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113) many labs have been caught performing very poor work resulting in false convictions or acquittals. For example, it was discovered after an audit of the Houston Police Department in 2002 that the lab had fabricated evidence which led George Rodriguez being convicted of raping a fourteen-year-old girl.[[114]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-114) The former director of the lab, when asked, said that the total number of cases that could have been contaminated by improper work could be in the range of 5,000 to 10,000.[[114]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-114)

The Innocence Project[[115]](#cite_note-115) database of DNA exonerations shows that many wrongful convictions contained forensic science errors. According to the Innocence project and the US Department of Justice, forensic science has contributed to about 39 percent to 46 percent of wrongful convictions.[[116]](#cite_note-116) As indicated by the National Academy of Sciences report *Strengthening Forensic Sciences in the United States*,[[111]](#cite_note-Nap.edu-2009-111) part of the problem is that many traditional forensic sciences have never been empirically validated; and part of the problem is that all examiners are subject to forensic confirmation biases and should be shielded from contextual information not relevant to the judgment they make.

Many studies have discovered a difference in rape-related injuries reporting based on race, with white victims reporting a higher frequency of injuries than black victims.[[117]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-117) However, since current forensic examination techniques may not be sensitive to all injuries across a range of skin colors, more research needs to be conducted to understand if this trend is due to skin confounding healthcare providers when examining injuries or if darker skin extends a protective element.[[117]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-117) In clinical practice, for patients with darker skin, one study recommends that attention must be paid to the thighs, labia majora, posterior fourchette and [fossa navicularis](/wiki/Fossa_of_vestibule_of_vagina "Fossa of vestibule of vagina"), so that no rape-related injuries are missed upon close examination.[[117]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-117)

## Forensic science and humanitarian work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Forensic_science&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Forensic science and humanitarian work")]

The [International Committee of the Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") (ICRC) uses forensic science for humanitarian purposes to clarify the fate of missing persons after armed conflict, disasters or migration,[[118]](#cite_note-118) and is one of the services related to [Restoring Family Links](/wiki/Restoring_Family_Links "Restoring Family Links") and Missing Persons. Knowing what has happened to a missing relative can often make it easier to proceed with the grieving process and move on with life for families of missing persons.

Forensic science is used by various other organizations to clarify the fate and whereabouts of persons who have gone missing. Examples include the NGO [Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team](/wiki/Argentine_Forensic_Anthropology_Team "Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team"), working to clarify the fate of people who disappeared during the period of the 1976–1983 military dictatorship. The [International Commission on Missing Persons](/wiki/International_Commission_on_Missing_Persons "International Commission on Missing Persons") (ICMP) used forensic science to find missing persons,[[119]](#cite_note-119) for example after the conflicts in the Balkans.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Recognising the role of forensic science for humanitarian purposes, as well as the importance of forensic investigations in fulfilling the state's responsibilities to investigate human rights violations, a group of experts in the late-1980s devised a UN Manual on the Prevention and Investigation of Extra-Legal, Arbitrary and Summary Executions, which became known as the [Minnesota Protocol](/wiki/Minnesota_Protocol "Minnesota Protocol"). This document was revised and re-published by the [Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights](/wiki/Office_of_the_High_Commissioner_for_Human_Rights "Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights") in 2016.[[121]](#cite_note-121)
